text: In 2006, researchers at University College London studied how scholars used online journals and e-book archives. The data revealed a striking pattern: users bounced between documents, rarely reading more than one or two pages of any source before moving on, often never returning. The researchers called this behavior 'power browsing' — a horizontal sweep across titles, abstracts, and first paragraphs in place of vertical immersion in a single text. What looked like increased access to knowledge was, in practice, a new mode of avoiding it. More documents were opened; fewer were actually read. Breadth had displaced depth so seamlessly that users appeared not to notice the substitution.
question: What term did UCL researchers use to describe the pattern of skimming titles and abstracts without reading full sources?
